subject: LU-398 ptlrpc: Add the NRS ORR and TRR policies

values.-1: I would prefer that you didn't submit this
values.+1: Looks good to me, but someone else must approve

Patch Set 1:

Build Started http://build.whamcloud.com/job/lustre-reviews/11567/

Patch Set 1: Fails

Build Failed 
 
http://build.whamcloud.com/job/lustre-reviews/11567/ : FAILURE

Patch Set 1:

This build is failing because it relies on the NRS framework patch being landed first; i will upload a new version tomorrow anyway, but i don't know how to get access to manually start/stop jenkins builds.

Patch Set 1: I would prefer that you didn't submit this

Nikitas,
if one patch depends on another in order to build or work, then they need to be submitted as a patch series.

That means they should be pushed from the same git branch on your side, with the NRS framework first and this patch as a second commit after that.

When Gerrit checks out this branch, it will also get the earlier patch, and everything should build/test correctly.

Patch Set 1:

NB - just marked -1 since this patch needs to be re-submitted for dependencies, I haven't actually looked at it yet.

Patch Set 1:

Thanks for letting me know Andreas, i will do this asap. From what i gather, keeping the existing change-IDs for the commits will enable Gerrit to pick the patches up even if they are in a series.
Uploaded patch set 2.
Uploaded patch set 3.

Patch Set 3:

Build Started http://build.whamcloud.com/job/lustre-reviews/11910/

Patch Set 3: Verified

Build Successful 
 
http://build.whamcloud.com/job/lustre-reviews/11910/ : SUCCESS

Patch Set 3:

This patch needs two things:

1. To add the new-style lprocfs functions (i.e. as per Liang's comments in the 'framework and fifo policy' patch, patchset 9, to have one lprocfs file per service, and not per service partition); I should have this ready and uploaded by the end of today.

2. Especially the ORR policy, and less so the TRR policy, need some way of recycling struct nrs_orr_object instances, otherwise given enough runtime, these will grow to unacceptable levels of memory usage. One way to get around this is to temporarily shut down and then restart the policy, such that all previously used resources are freed, but that cannot be a good solution.

I was not sure whether this mechanism should be LRU-based, a simple freeing of each object when its usage counter drops to zero, or something else, so any suggestions are more than welcome.

However, I am hoping that if we _really_ need to, we could perhaps land this patch without having addressed #2 entirely, and it would be ok to submit an extra patch for that after the feature window closes, since minor enhancements and bugfixes are meant to be accepted for a few weeks after the feature window will close, and this shouldn't be too big a change.

Patch Set 3:

I'd really prefer to avoid landing the patch with (essentially) a memory leak.  The only saving grace might be that this policy is not active by default, though I imagine it would cause a problem for any real-world usage for an extended time.

I think it makes sense to free the struct as soon as the refcount drops to zero, since it isn't clear whether an object will ever be modified again after the initial set of RPCs finishes.

Uploaded patch set 9.

Patch Set 9:

For the TRR policy, I think the need to free its nrs_orr_object instances may be almost non-existent (each of these objects corresponds to one OST in the OSS in this policy), as the set of OSTs on an OSS should remain stable for long periods of uptime; but maybe there are special use cases which I am not considering. In any case, it may be best if there is a mechanism for freeing these (I have added the same as for ORR for now), but again I am trying to avoid adding overhead where it can be safely avoided. 

Another thing that could potentially be avoided is the use of a libcfs hash instance to hash OST indices (for the TRR policy), as for such small sets using a hash may involve an unnecessary overhead compaired to a simpler data structure. Sorry to ask this, but could someone please review the patch and offer some advice?

Patch Set 13:

I've considered in the past to return the st_rdev field in fe_debixe for local filesystems, so that multi-device filesystems can distinguish between blocks on two different devices. So I think it would make sense to do either this, or have the OSDs insert the index so that cola are not incorrectly sorted across multiple disks.

Uploaded patch set 22.

Patch Set 22:

I have changed the ORR policy to use the backend-fs object's OST FID as the scheduling key for uniquely identifying objects to schedule RPCs against, instead of the combination of OST index + object id, as per an earlier comment by Andreas, where he points out that DNE-allocated objects may share the same OST index and object id, which might lead to suboptimal performance if there are too many clashes; this also makes the code more in-line with other 2.4 additions. The TRR policy still uses only the OST index as the scheduling key.

commit.subject: LU-398 ptlrpc: Add the NRS ORR and TRR policies
commit.message:
LU-398 ptlrpc: Add the NRS ORR and TRR policies

The ORR (Object-based Round Robin) policy schedules brw RPCs in
per-backend-filesystem-object groupings; RPCs in each group are
sorted according to their logical file or physical disk offsets.

The TRR (Target-based Round Robin) policy performs the same
function as ORR, but instead schedules brw RPCs in per-OST
groupings.

Both these policies aim to provide for increased read throughput
in certain use cases, either by minimizing costly disk seek
operations (by ordering OST_READ, and perhaps also OST_WRITE
RPCs), but may also allow for improved performance through better
resource utilization and by taking advantage of locality of
reference characteristics of the I/O load.

Signed-off-by: Nikitas Angelinas <nikitas_angelinas@xyratex.com>
Co-authored-by: Liang Zhen <liang@whamcloud.com>
Change-Id: I1f5a367f2f4a1cf296a3b38f3e395ab28a10668e
Oracle-bug-id: b=13634
Xyratex-bug-id: MRP-73
